title: Provision Core Infrastructure (Bolt - Kubernetes/Helm)
description: Deploys and configures the foundational infrastructure components required by all backend services, including PostgreSQL, Redis/Valkey, and S3/R2 bucket configurations. This task establishes the database and caching layers in the Kubernetes cluster.
details: {"steps":["1. Create a new Kubernetes namespace 'sigma1'.","2. Using Helm, install the CloudNative-PG operator into the cluster.","3. Define and apply a 'Cluster' Custom Resource named 'sigma1-postgres' in the 'sigma1' namespace with a 50Gi storage request and initial 'sigma1' database.","4. Using Helm, install the Valkey/Redis operator.","5. Define and apply a 'Redis' Custom Resource named 'sigma1-valkey' in the 'sigma1' namespace.","6. Create a Kubernetes secret 'sigma1-db-secret' containing the superuser credentials from the CloudNative-PG-generated secret.","7. Create a ConfigMap 'sigma1-infra-endpoints' to store connection URLs for Postgres (POSTGRES_URL) and Redis (REDIS_URL) for other services to consume.","8. Configure IAM policies and create an S3/R2 bucket named 'sigma1-assets' for image and photo storage."]}
test_strategy: Verify that the 'sigma1-postgres' cluster is healthy using `kubectl get cluster sigma1-postgres -n sigma1`. Confirm the 'sigma1-valkey' Redis instance is running. Check that the 'sigma1-infra-endpoints' ConfigMap exists and contains valid-looking connection strings.

decision_points.description: How should data be isolated for the various backend services (RMS, Finance, Catalog, etc.) within the single PostgreSQL cluster?
options: Use a single PostgreSQL database with logically separated schemas (e.g., `rms`, `finance`, `catalog`) for each service, as hinted in the PRD.
Provision separate, physically isolated PostgreSQL databases for each service to enforce stronger microservice boundaries and prevent data coupling.

decision_points.description: Which object storage provider should be used for storing product images and social media content?
options: Use Cloudflare R2, leveraging its integration with the Cloudflare CDN and Pages hosting to minimize egress costs.
Use AWS S3 for its mature ecosystem, extensive tooling, and established industry-standard APIs.

decision_points.description: Which Redis-compatible engine should be used for caching and rate-limiting?
options: Use Valkey, as specified in the example Kubernetes manifest, aligning with the open-source fork of Redis.
Use a standard Redis deployment, which has broader community support and library compatibility.

subtasks.title: Implement Provision Core Infrastructure (Bolt - Kubernetes/Helm)
subtasks.description: Deploys and configures the foundational infrastructure components required by all backend services, including PostgreSQL, Redis/Valkey, and S3/R2 bucket configurations. This task establishes the database and caching layers in the Kubernetes cluster.
subtasks.details: {"steps":["1. Create a new Kubernetes namespace 'sigma1'.","2. Using Helm, install the CloudNative-PG operator into the cluster.","3. Define and apply a 'Cluster' Custom Resource named 'sigma1-postgres' in the 'sigma1' namespace with a 50Gi storage request and initial 'sigma1' database.","4. Using Helm, install the Valkey/Redis operator.","5. Define and apply a 'Redis' Custom Resource named 'sigma1-valkey' in the 'sigma1' namespace.","6. Create a Kubernetes secret 'sigma1-db-secret' containing the superuser credentials from the CloudNative-PG-generated secret.","7. Create a ConfigMap 'sigma1-infra-endpoints' to store connection URLs for Postgres (POSTGRES_URL) and Redis (REDIS_URL) for other services to consume.","8. Configure IAM policies and create an S3/R2 bucket named 'sigma1-assets' for image and photo storage."]}
subtasks.test_strategy: Verify that the 'sigma1-postgres' cluster is healthy using `kubectl get cluster sigma1-postgres -n sigma1`. Confirm the 'sigma1-valkey' Redis instance is running. Check that the 'sigma1-infra-endpoints' ConfigMap exists and contains valid-looking connection strings.

title: Bootstrap Equipment Catalog Service (Rex - Rust/Axum)
description: Creates the initial Rust project for the Equipment Catalog service. This includes setting up the Axum web server, defining core data models for products and categories, and implementing initial read-only API endpoints.
details: {"steps":["1. Initialize a new Rust binary project using `cargo new equipment-catalog`.","2. Add dependencies: `axum`, `tokio`, `sqlx` (with `postgres`, `runtime-tokio-rustls`, `uuid`, `decimal`, `json` features), `serde`, `uuid`, `rust_decimal`.","3. Create `src/models.rs` and define the `Product` and `Category` structs based on the PRD, using `Uuid`, `Decimal`, and `serde_json::Value` for specs.","4. In `src/main.rs`, set up a basic Axum router with `/health/live` and `/health/ready` endpoints.","5. Implement the `GET /api/v1/catalog/products` endpoint. It should connect to the PostgreSQL database (using the URL from 'sigma1-infra-endpoints') and return a static, empty JSON array `[]` for now.","6. Create a `Dockerfile` for building a statically-linked, minimal Rust executable using a multi-stage build.","7. Create a basic Helm chart for deploying the service to Kubernetes, ensuring it mounts the 'sigma1-infra-endpoints' ConfigMap as environment variables."]}
test_strategy: After deploying the service, `curl` the `/api/v1/catalog/products` endpoint and verify it returns a `200 OK` status with the JSON body `[]`. The `/health/live` endpoint must also return `200 OK`.

decision_points.description: How should data be isolated for the various backend services (RMS, Finance, Catalog, etc.) within the single PostgreSQL cluster?
options: Use a single PostgreSQL database with logically separated schemas (e.g., `rms`, `finance`, `catalog`) for each service, as hinted in the PRD.
Provision separate, physically isolated PostgreSQL databases for each service to enforce stronger microservice boundaries and prevent data coupling.

decision_points.description: Which object storage provider should be used for storing product images and social media content?
options: Use Cloudflare R2, leveraging its integration with the Cloudflare CDN and Pages hosting to minimize egress costs.
Use AWS S3 for its mature ecosystem, extensive tooling, and established industry-standard APIs.

decision_points.description: What should be the primary communication protocol for internal service-to-service calls?
options: Standardize on gRPC for all internal communication to maximize performance and type safety, using grpc-gateway for external REST exposure where needed.
Embrace a mixed-protocol approach: use gRPC for high-throughput services like RMS and simple REST/JSON for other services like Catalog and Finance, as currently implied by the PRD.

decision_points.description: How will authentication and authorization be handled for service-to-service calls and external API access from the frontend?
options: Implement service-to-service authentication using short-lived JWTs issued by a central identity service, which would also issue tokens for the frontend client.
Use a service mesh with mTLS for encrypted and authenticated service-to-service communication, and use JWTs for external API clients.

decision_points.description: How will database schema migrations be managed across the multiple services using the PostgreSQL cluster?
options: Each service will manage its own schema migrations independently using a language-specific tool (e.g., `sqlx-cli` for Rust, `golang-migrate` for Go).
Use a single, language-agnostic migration tool like Flyway or Liquibase, managed from a central repository, to provide a unified view and control over all database changes.

decision_points.description: Which Redis-compatible engine should be used for caching and rate-limiting?
options: Use Valkey, as specified in the example Kubernetes manifest, aligning with the open-source fork of Redis.
Use a standard Redis deployment, which has broader community support and library compatibility.

title: Bootstrap RMS Service & gRPC Definitions (Grizz - Go/gRPC)
description: Initializes the Go project for the Rental Management System (RMS) and defines the core gRPC services and messages using Protocol Buffers. This task focuses on scaffolding the Opportunity and Project services.
details: {"steps":["1. Initialize a new Go module: `go mod init github.com/sigma1/rms`.","2. Create a `proto/` directory for protobuf definitions.","3. Create `proto/opportunity.proto` and define the `OpportunityService` with its associated messages (`Opportunity`, `CreateOpportunityRequest`, etc.) as specified in the PRD.","4. Create `proto/project.proto` and define the `ProjectService` and its messages.","5. Use `protoc` with `protoc-gen-go` and `protoc-gen-go-grpc` to generate the Go gRPC server and client code from the `.proto` files.","6. In `cmd/server/main.go`, implement a basic gRPC server that registers the `OpportunityService` and `ProjectService`.","7. Implement a mock `CreateOpportunity` RPC handler that logs the request and returns a hardcoded `Opportunity` response.","8. Create a `Dockerfile` and a Helm chart for deployment, referencing the 'sigma1-infra-endpoints' ConfigMap."]}
test_strategy: Run the gRPC server. Use `grpcurl` to call the `OpportunityService.CreateOpportunity` method with a valid request payload. Verify that the server logs the request and returns the expected hardcoded response without errors.

options: Use a single PostgreSQL database with logically separated schemas (e.g., `rms`, `finance`, `catalog`) for each service, as hinted in the PRD.

title: Bootstrap Finance Service (Rex - Rust/Axum)
description: Creates the initial Rust project for the Finance service. This includes setting up the Axum web server, defining the core `Invoice` and `Payment` data models, and implementing a placeholder endpoint for invoice creation.
details: {"steps":["1. Initialize a new Rust binary project: `cargo new finance-service`.","2. Add dependencies similar to the Equipment Catalog service (`axum`, `sqlx`, `serde`, etc.).","3. Create `src/models.rs` and define the `Invoice`, `Payment`, and `InvoiceStatus` structs/enums as specified in the PRD.","4. In `src/main.rs`, set up an Axum router with health check endpoints.","5. Implement a placeholder `POST /api/v1/invoices` endpoint that accepts a valid JSON body, logs it, and returns a `201 Created` status with a hardcoded response.","6. Set up database connection pooling using `sqlx::PgPool` configured from environment variables.","7. Create a `Dockerfile` and a Helm chart for deployment, referencing the 'sigma1-infra-endpoints' ConfigMap."]}
test_strategy: Deploy the service. Send a `POST` request to `/api/v1/invoices` with a valid JSON payload representing a new invoice. Verify the API returns a `201 Created` status code and the correct hardcoded JSON response.

decision_points.description: How should data be isolated for the various backend services (RMS, Finance, Catalog, etc.) within the single PostgreSQL cluster?
options: Use a single PostgreSQL database with logically separated schemas (e.g., `rms`, `finance`, `catalog`) for each service, as hinted in the PRD.
Provision separate, physically isolated PostgreSQL databases for each service to enforce stronger microservice boundaries and prevent data coupling.

title: Bootstrap Customer Vetting Service (Rex - Rust/Axum)
description: Creates the initial Rust project for the Customer Vetting service. This includes setting up the Axum web server, defining the `VettingResult` data model, and implementing the initial `POST /api/v1/vetting/run` endpoint.
details: {"steps":["1. Initialize a new Rust binary project: `cargo new customer-vetting-service`.","2. Add standard dependencies (`axum`, `sqlx`, `serde`, etc.).","3. Create `src/models.rs` and define the `VettingResult` and `LeadScore` structs/enums as specified in the PRD.","4. In `src/main.rs`, set up an Axum router with health check endpoints.","5. Implement the `POST /api/v1/vetting/run` endpoint. It should accept an organization ID, log it, and return a `202 Accepted` status immediately. The actual pipeline will be implemented later.","6. Implement the `GET /api/v1/vetting/:org_id` endpoint to return a hardcoded `VettingResult` with a `final_score` of `YELLOW`.","7. Create a `Dockerfile` and a Helm chart for deployment."]}
test_strategy: Send a `POST` request to `/api/v1/vetting/run`. Verify a `202 Accepted` response. Then, send a `GET` request to `/api/v1/vetting/some-org-id` and verify it returns a `200 OK` with the hardcoded `VettingResult` JSON object.

decision_points.description: How should data be isolated for the various backend services (RMS, Finance, Catalog, etc.) within the single PostgreSQL cluster?
options: Use a single PostgreSQL database with logically separated schemas (e.g., `rms`, `finance`, `catalog`) for each service, as hinted in the PRD.
Provision separate, physically isolated PostgreSQL databases for each service to enforce stronger microservice boundaries and prevent data coupling.

decision_points.description: What should be the primary communication protocol for internal service-to-service calls?
options: Standardize on gRPC for all internal communication to maximize performance and type safety, using grpc-gateway for external REST exposure where needed.
Embrace a mixed-protocol approach: use gRPC for high-throughput services like RMS and simple REST/JSON for other services like Catalog and Finance, as currently implied by the PRD.

decision_points.description: How will authentication and authorization be handled for service-to-service calls and external API access from the frontend?
options: Implement service-to-service authentication using short-lived JWTs issued by a central identity service, which would also issue tokens for the frontend client.
Use a service mesh with mTLS for encrypted and authenticated service-to-service communication, and use JWTs for external API clients.

decision_points.description: What architectural pattern should be used for orchestrating long-running, multi-step business workflows like customer vetting and social media publishing?
options: Use an event-driven approach with the existing in-cluster NATS message queue to decouple services and manage asynchronous workflows.
Implement a direct orchestration pattern where the Morgan agent makes a series of synchronous API calls to other services to complete the workflow.

decision_points.description: How will the Customer Vetting service source Google Reviews data?
options: Build an in-house web scraping solution to gather Google Reviews data, minimizing direct costs but incurring maintenance overhead.
Use a paid, third-party data provider API for reliable access to Google Reviews data, increasing cost but reducing maintenance and improving reliability.

decision_points.description: How will database schema migrations be managed across the multiple services using the PostgreSQL cluster?
options: Each service will manage its own schema migrations independently using a language-specific tool (e.g., `sqlx-cli` for Rust, `golang-migrate` for Go).
Use a single, language-agnostic migration tool like Flyway or Liquibase, managed from a central repository, to provide a unified view and control over all database changes.

title: Bootstrap Social Media Engine (Nova - Bun/Elysia)
description: Initializes the Node.js project for the Social Media Engine using the Bun runtime and Elysia framework. This task sets up the basic server, defines routes, and integrates Effect for type-safe schema validation.
details: {"steps":["1. Initialize a new Bun project: `bun init`.","2. Add dependencies: `elysia`, `@effect/schema`, `@effect/core`.","3. Set up a basic Elysia server in `src/index.ts`.","4. Define the API routes from the PRD (`/api/v1/social/...`) using Elysia's router.","5. Implement a placeholder for `GET /api/v1/social/drafts` that returns an empty array `[]`.","6. Use `@effect/schema` to define a schema for a `SocialPostDraft` and use it in the response model for the drafts endpoint to ensure type safety.","7. Create a `Dockerfile` that uses the `oven/bun` base image.","8. Create a Helm chart for deployment."]}
test_strategy: After deploying the service, send a `GET` request to `/api/v1/social/drafts`. Verify the response is `200 OK` with a JSON body of `[]`.

title: Setup Website & Equipment Catalog Pages (Blaze - React/Next.js)
description: Initializes the Next.js 14 project for the Sigma-1 website. This task includes setting up the project structure, TailwindCSS, shadcn/ui, and building the main equipment browsing pages that fetch data from the Equipment Catalog service.
details: {"steps":["1. Create a new Next.js project using `create-next-app` with the App Router, TypeScript, and TailwindCSS.","2. Initialize `shadcn/ui` by running `npx shadcn-ui@latest init`.","3. Add `tanstack-query` for data fetching.","4. Create the page route `/equipment` which will list all product categories.","5. Create the dynamic page route `/equipment/[category]` to list products within a category.","6. Implement data fetching logic using TanStack Query to call the `GET /api/v1/catalog/categories` and `GET /api/v1/catalog/products` endpoints from the Equipment Catalog service.","7. Use `shadcn/ui` components like `Card` and `Button` to build a clean, responsive layout for displaying products.","8. Create a placeholder `/llms.txt` file in the `public` directory."]}
test_strategy: Run the Next.js development server. Navigate to the `/equipment` page. The page should render correctly and display product data fetched from the live Equipment Catalog API. All links should be functional and the layout must be responsive.

decision_points.description: How will authentication and authorization be handled for service-to-service calls and external API access from the frontend?
options: Implement service-to-service authentication using short-lived JWTs issued by a central identity service, which would also issue tokens for the frontend client.
Use a service mesh with mTLS for encrypted and authenticated service-to-service communication, and use JWTs for external API clients.

decision_points.description: How will the Morgan web chat widget be implemented and integrated into the Next.js site?
options: Build a custom React component for the chat widget from scratch, providing full control over its appearance and behavior.
Source a third-party, embeddable chat widget library and connect it to the Morgan agent's backend, accelerating development but limiting customization.

subtasks.title: Implement Setup Website & Equipment Catalog Pages (Blaze - React/Next.js)
subtasks.description: Initializes the Next.js 14 project for the Sigma-1 website. This task includes setting up the project structure, TailwindCSS, shadcn/ui, and building the main equipment browsing pages that fetch data from the Equipment Catalog service.
subtasks.details: {"steps":["1. Create a new Next.js project using `create-next-app` with the App Router, TypeScript, and TailwindCSS.","2. Initialize `shadcn/ui` by running `npx shadcn-ui@latest init`.","3. Add `tanstack-query` for data fetching.","4. Create the page route `/equipment` which will list all product categories.","5. Create the dynamic page route `/equipment/[category]` to list products within a category.","6. Implement data fetching logic using TanStack Query to call the `GET /api/v1/catalog/categories` and `GET /api/v1/catalog/products` endpoints from the Equipment Catalog service.","7. Use `shadcn/ui` components like `Card` and `Button` to build a clean, responsive layout for displaying products.","8. Create a placeholder `/llms.txt` file in the `public` directory."]}
subtasks.test_strategy: Run the Next.js development server. Navigate to the `/equipment` page. The page should render correctly and display product data fetched from the live Equipment Catalog API. All links should be functional and the layout must be responsive.

title: Bootstrap Morgan AI Agent & Catalog Tool (Angie - OpenClaw)
description: Sets up the basic configuration for the Morgan AI agent within the OpenClaw framework. This includes defining the agent's core personality, creating its persistent workspace, and implementing the first MCP tool: `sigma1_catalog_search`.
details: {"steps":["1. Create a new directory for the agent: `agents/morgan`.","2. Create `agent.toml` defining Morgan's ID, model (e.g., `openai-api/gpt-4o`), and core system prompt.","3. In the central `tool-server`, create a new tool file `tools/sigma1.py`.","4. Implement the `sigma1_catalog_search` tool. This function should accept a search query string.","5. The tool will make an HTTP GET request to the Equipment Catalog service's `/api/v1/catalog/products?q=<query>` endpoint.","6. The tool should parse the JSON response and return a formatted string summarizing the found products.","7. Create a Kubernetes Deployment manifest for Morgan, including a PersistentVolumeClaim for its workspace.","8. Configure the deployment to connect to the tool-server."]}
test_strategy: Deploy the Morgan agent and tool-server. Use the OpenClaw CLI or a test script to invoke the `sigma1_catalog_search` tool with a query like 'mixer'. Verify that the tool successfully calls the Equipment Catalog API and returns a formatted string of search results.

decision_points.description: What should be the primary communication protocol for internal service-to-service calls?
options: Standardize on gRPC for all internal communication to maximize performance and type safety, using grpc-gateway for external REST exposure where needed.
Embrace a mixed-protocol approach: use gRPC for high-throughput services like RMS and simple REST/JSON for other services like Catalog and Finance, as currently implied by the PRD.

decision_points.description: How will authentication and authorization be handled for service-to-service calls and external API access from the frontend?
options: Implement service-to-service authentication using short-lived JWTs issued by a central identity service, which would also issue tokens for the frontend client.
Use a service mesh with mTLS for encrypted and authenticated service-to-service communication, and use JWTs for external API clients.

decision_points.description: What architectural pattern should be used for orchestrating long-running, multi-step business workflows like customer vetting and social media publishing?
options: Use an event-driven approach with the existing in-cluster NATS message queue to decouple services and manage asynchronous workflows.
Implement a direct orchestration pattern where the Morgan agent makes a series of synchronous API calls to other services to complete the workflow.

decision_points.description: How should the Signal-CLI dependency for the Morgan agent be deployed?
options: Deploy Signal-CLI as a sidecar container within the same Kubernetes pod as the Morgan agent for simplified networking and co-location.
Deploy Signal-CLI as a separate, dedicated service in the cluster, allowing it to be managed and scaled independently from the Morgan agent.

decision_points.description: How will the Morgan web chat widget be implemented and integrated into the Next.js site?
options: Build a custom React component for the chat widget from scratch, providing full control over its appearance and behavior.
Source a third-party, embeddable chat widget library and connect it to the Morgan agent's backend, accelerating development but limiting customization.

title: Establish CI/CD Pipeline Foundations (Atlas - CI/CD)
description: Creates the initial CI/CD pipelines for all services using GitHub Actions. Each service (Rust, Go, Node.js, Next.js) will have a dedicated workflow that triggers on pull requests to run linting, formatting checks, and unit tests.
details: {"steps":["1. Create the `.github/workflows` directory in the monorepo root.","2. Create `rust-ci.yml`. This workflow should trigger on PRs targeting Rust service directories. It will run `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`, and `cargo test`.","3. Create `go-ci.yml`. This workflow will trigger on PRs for the RMS service. It will run `go fmt`, `go vet`, and `go test ./...`.","4. Create `node-ci.yml`. This workflow will trigger on PRs for the Social Media Engine. It will run `bunx biome check` and `bun test`.","5. Create `nextjs-ci.yml`. This workflow will trigger on PRs for the website. It will run `npm run lint`, `npm run typecheck`, and `npm run build`.","6. Configure path filters for each workflow to ensure they only run when relevant code changes."]}
test_strategy: Create a new pull request for one of the Rust services with a deliberate formatting error. Verify that the `rust-ci.yml` workflow is triggered and that the `cargo fmt --check` step fails as expected, blocking the PR.

subtasks.title: Implement Establish CI/CD Pipeline Foundations (Atlas - CI/CD)
subtasks.description: Creates the initial CI/CD pipelines for all services using GitHub Actions. Each service (Rust, Go, Node.js, Next.js) will have a dedicated workflow that triggers on pull requests to run linting, formatting checks, and unit tests.
subtasks.details: {"steps":["1. Create the `.github/workflows` directory in the monorepo root.","2. Create `rust-ci.yml`. This workflow should trigger on PRs targeting Rust service directories. It will run `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`, and `cargo test`.","3. Create `go-ci.yml`. This workflow will trigger on PRs for the RMS service. It will run `go fmt`, `go vet`, and `go test ./...`.","4. Create `node-ci.yml`. This workflow will trigger on PRs for the Social Media Engine. It will run `bunx biome check` and `bun test`.","5. Create `nextjs-ci.yml`. This workflow will trigger on PRs for the website. It will run `npm run lint`, `npm run typecheck`, and `npm run build`.","6. Configure path filters for each workflow to ensure they only run when relevant code changes."]}
subtasks.test_strategy: Create a new pull request for one of the Rust services with a deliberate formatting error. Verify that the `rust-ci.yml` workflow is triggered and that the `cargo fmt --check` step fails as expected, blocking the PR.

title: Create Integration Test for Lead Qualification (Tess - Test frameworks)
description: Develops the first end-to-end integration test to validate the 'Inbound Lead -> Qualified Opportunity' data flow (DF-1). This test will simulate an inbound lead and verify that the correct services are called in sequence.
details: {"steps":["1. Set up a new testing package/directory (e.g., using a Go test suite or Playwright) for integration tests.","2. The test will require a running environment with the Morgan Agent, Customer Vetting Service, and RMS Service deployed.","3. Write a test case that simulates an inbound lead by making a request to a mock Signal/Morgan endpoint.","4. Use a tool like `mitmproxy` or service mocks to intercept and verify the outbound calls from Morgan.","5. Assert that Morgan first calls the Customer Vetting service's `/api/v1/vetting/run` endpoint.","6. Assert that Morgan subsequently calls the RMS service's `OpportunityService.CreateOpportunity` or `ScoreLead` gRPC method.","7. The test passes if both service calls are made in the correct order with the expected payloads."]}
test_strategy: Execute the integration test suite against a deployed test environment. The test runner must report a 'PASS' status, confirming that the services for the lead qualification flow are correctly orchestrated by the Morgan agent.

options: Standardize on gRPC for all internal communication to maximize performance and type safety, using grpc-gateway for external REST exposure where needed.

options: Use an event-driven approach with the existing in-cluster NATS message queue to decouple services and manage asynchronous workflows.
Implement a direct orchestration pattern where the Morgan agent makes a series of synchronous API calls to other services to complete the workflow.
